prompt: Which choice best states how Text 2 challenges Text 1?

choices.text: it argues that a single letter grade conceals what a student can really do
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2's key challenge is that a letter hides actual ability.

choices.text: it says that students ought not to receive any feedback on their work
choices.rationale: Text 2 favors detailed feedback.

choices.text: it claims that employers ignore academic records of every kind entirely
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not make a claim about employers ignoring records.

choices.text: it agrees that letter grades capture a student's understanding perfectly
choices.rationale: Text 2 disputes that grades capture understanding.

explanation: Text 2 challenges Text 1 by noting a letter conceals real ability.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Letter grades give students clear targets and tell colleges and employers, at a glance, how a student performed. That clarity, the author argues, is hard to replace.
stimulus.text_b: Grades, this author counters, can push students to chase points instead of understanding, and a single letter hides what a learner can actually do. Detailed feedback, the text says, teaches more.

prompt: Based on the texts, the two authors most clearly disagree about whether

choices.text: letter grades are an effective way to represent a student's learning
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 praises grades' clarity; Text 2 says they distort and hide learning.

choices.text: written feedback can, as a practical matter, be recorded on paper
choices.rationale: Both accept feedback can be written.

choices.text: students physically attend a school building during the academic year
choices.rationale: Both assume students attend school.

choices.text: colleges receive any applications at all from prospective students
choices.rationale: Both accept colleges get applications.

explanation: The dispute is whether letter grades effectively represent learning.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Letter grades give students clear targets and tell colleges and employers, at a glance, how a student performed. That clarity, the author argues, is hard to replace.
stimulus.text_b: Grades, this author counters, can push students to chase points instead of understanding, and a single letter hides what a learner can actually do. Detailed feedback, the text says, teaches more.

prompt: The author of Text 2 would most likely respond to Text 1's emphasis on economic benefit by

choices.text: denying that tourism creates any new jobs for local residents at all
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not deny job creation.

choices.text: noting that unchecked growth can erode a town's appeal and its affordability
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 warns of rising rents, crowding, and fading charm.

choices.text: claiming that small towns contain no shops for visitors to spend at
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not claim towns lack shops.

choices.text: agreeing that a larger number of visitors is always better for a town
choices.rationale: Text 2 warns against limitless growth.

explanation: Text 2 counters with the costs of unlimited growth.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Tourism brings jobs and money to small towns, the author observes, funding restaurants, guides, and shops that might not otherwise survive. Visitors, the text says, can keep a struggling community alive.
stimulus.text_b: When crowds overwhelm a small place, this author argues, rents rise, streets clog, and the very charm visitors came for begins to fade. Growth without limits, the text warns, can hollow a town out.

prompt: Based on the texts, the authors would most likely disagree about whether

choices.text: the restaurants in a small town employ any workers at all
choices.rationale: Both accept restaurants employ people.

choices.text: tourism is, on the whole, good for a small town's wellbeing
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 sees tourism as a lifeline; Text 2 warns it can hollow a town out.

choices.text: the visitors who come to a town actually spend any money there
choices.rationale: Both accept tourists spend money.

choices.text: a typical small town has streets that visitors can walk along
choices.rationale: Both accept towns have streets.

explanation: Their dispute is the net effect of tourism on a town.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Tourism brings jobs and money to small towns, the author observes, funding restaurants, guides, and shops that might not otherwise survive. Visitors, the text says, can keep a struggling community alive.
stimulus.text_b: When crowds overwhelm a small place, this author argues, rents rise, streets clog, and the very charm visitors came for begins to fade. Growth without limits, the text warns, can hollow a town out.

prompt: How would the author of Text 2 most likely respond to Text 1's concern about 'a link to the past'?

choices.text: by claiming that students today are simply unable to learn how to type
choices.rationale: Text 2 favors teaching typing.

choices.text: by agreeing that cursive should be the main focus of writing instruction
choices.rationale: Text 2 would not center cursive.

choices.text: by granting the value but arguing that limited time better serves practical writing
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 weighs the trade-off and prioritizes practical writing.

choices.text: by denying that the past holds any value or interest for students at all
choices.rationale: Text 2 calls cursive 'charming,' not worthless.

explanation: Text 2 would acknowledge the value yet prioritize practical skills.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Teaching cursive, the author maintains, trains fine motor skills, lets students read historical documents, and gives them a personal, fluent hand. Dropping it, the text warns, cuts a link to the past.
stimulus.text_b: Class time is limited, and this author argues it is better spent on typing and clear print, the writing students will actually use. Cursive, the text suggests, has become a charming but optional skill.

prompt: Based on the texts, the two authors most clearly disagree about whether

choices.text: the practice of typing on a keyboard genuinely exists today
choices.rationale: Both accept typing exists.

choices.text: students ought to learn to write down their thoughts at all
choices.rationale: Both assume students write.

choices.text: learning cursive deserves a share of limited classroom time
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 defends teaching cursive; Text 2 would spend the time elsewhere.

choices.text: any historical documents were ever written by hand in cursive
choices.rationale: Both accept historical documents exist.

explanation: The disagreement is whether cursive warrants class time.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Teaching cursive, the author maintains, trains fine motor skills, lets students read historical documents, and gives them a personal, fluent hand. Dropping it, the text warns, cuts a link to the past.
stimulus.text_b: Class time is limited, and this author argues it is better spent on typing and clear print, the writing students will actually use. Cursive, the text suggests, has become a charming but optional skill.

prompt: Which choice best describes how Text 2 relates to Text 1?

choices.text: it argues that the wolves should now be removed from the park
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not call for removing wolves.

choices.text: it supplies precise numerical proof for the explanation in Text 1
choices.rationale: Text 2 offers caution, not numerical proof.

choices.text: it restates the explanation in Text 1 without changing anything at all
choices.rationale: Text 2 adds nuance rather than restating.

choices.text: it complicates the explanation in Text 1 by adding several other causes
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 introduces additional factors that complicate the single-cause story.

explanation: Text 2 complicates Text 1 by adding multiple causes.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Reintroducing wolves to a national park, the author argues, restored balance: with deer no longer overgrazing, young trees returned, and with them came birds and beavers. A single predator, the text says, revived a whole landscape.
stimulus.text_b: Crediting wolves alone for the park's recovery oversimplifies a messy story, this author cautions. Weather, other species, and human management all shifted at once, making the wolves' exact role hard to isolate.

prompt: Based on the texts, the author of Text 2 would most likely characterize the claim in Text 1 as

choices.text: completely correct in every detail and in need of no qualification at all
choices.rationale: Text 2 questions, not endorses, the claim's completeness.

choices.text: overstated, because several other factors also contributed to the recovery
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 says crediting wolves alone oversimplifies a multi-cause story.

choices.text: clear proof that the reintroduced wolves had no real effect on the park
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not deny wolves had any effect.

choices.text: evidence that the park's deer had never actually been overgrazing at all
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not dispute overgrazing.

explanation: Text 2 sees Text 1's single-cause claim as overstated.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Reintroducing wolves to a national park, the author argues, restored balance: with deer no longer overgrazing, young trees returned, and with them came birds and beavers. A single predator, the text says, revived a whole landscape.
stimulus.text_b: Crediting wolves alone for the park's recovery oversimplifies a messy story, this author cautions. Weather, other species, and human management all shifted at once, making the wolves' exact role hard to isolate.

prompt: The author of Text 2 would most likely point to which idea as a weakness of the view in Text 1?

choices.text: employees are, in practice, not free to choose where they live
choices.rationale: Text 1 says workers can choose where to live.

choices.text: being productive while working from home is entirely impossible
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not claim productivity is impossible, only that some collaboration suffers.

choices.text: casual in-person conversations that spark new ideas may be lost
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 warns that remote work weakens idea-sparking conversations.

choices.text: the commutes that workers face are simply too short to matter
choices.rationale: Text 1 stresses skipping long commutes, not short ones.

explanation: Text 2's key concern is the loss of spontaneous collaboration.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Remote work lets employees skip long commutes, focus without office noise, and live where they choose. The author points to surveys in which workers report higher satisfaction and steady productivity.
stimulus.text_b: Working from home, the author warns, can blur the line between job and rest and weaken the casual conversations that spark new ideas. Some collaboration, this text argues, happens best in person.

prompt: Based on the texts, how do the authors differ in their overall view of remote work?

choices.text: both authors conclude that remote work ought to be banned outright
choices.rationale: Neither calls for a ban.

choices.text: Text 1 emphasizes its benefits, while Text 2 emphasizes its drawbacks
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 highlights benefits; Text 2 highlights drawbacks.

choices.text: both authors conclude that remote work has essentially no downsides
choices.rationale: Text 2 names downsides.

choices.text: Text 1 firmly opposes remote work, while Text 2 strongly supports it
choices.rationale: Their stances are reversed in this option.

explanation: Text 1 stresses benefits; Text 2 stresses drawbacks.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Remote work lets employees skip long commutes, focus without office noise, and live where they choose. The author points to surveys in which workers report higher satisfaction and steady productivity.
stimulus.text_b: Working from home, the author warns, can blur the line between job and rest and weaken the casual conversations that spark new ideas. Some collaboration, this text argues, happens best in person.

prompt: The author of Text 2 would most likely respond to Text 1's emphasis on convenience by

choices.text: agreeing that printed books should be abandoned in the classroom
choices.rationale: Text 2 wants to keep print central.

choices.text: acknowledging the convenience but giving priority to comprehension
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 values comprehension over the conveniences Text 1 cites.

choices.text: denying outright that screens are able to store many texts at once
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not dispute storage capacity.

choices.text: claiming that the font size of screen text can never be adjusted
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not make that claim.

explanation: Text 2 would grant the convenience yet stress comprehension.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Reading on screens lets students carry an entire library, search text instantly, and adjust font size for comfort. For many learners, the author notes, these tools make reading more accessible, not less.
stimulus.text_b: Studies suggest readers often comprehend and remember more from print than from screens, perhaps because paper invites slower, more focused attention. The author urges keeping print central in classrooms.

prompt: Based on the texts, the two authors would most likely disagree about whether

choices.text: reading on a screen is as good as print for the purpose of learning
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 praises screens; Text 2 favors print for comprehension.

choices.text: a typical library building actually contains physical books
choices.rationale: Both accept libraries hold books.

choices.text: the font size of text can be adjusted on an electronic screen
choices.rationale: Both would accept that screens adjust font size.

choices.text: students should be permitted to read texts of any kind at all
choices.rationale: Both assume students read.

explanation: The dispute is whether screens match print for learning.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Reading on screens lets students carry an entire library, search text instantly, and adjust font size for comfort. For many learners, the author notes, these tools make reading more accessible, not less.
stimulus.text_b: Studies suggest readers often comprehend and remember more from print than from screens, perhaps because paper invites slower, more focused attention. The author urges keeping print central in classrooms.

prompt: How does Text 2 respond to a central benefit claimed in Text 1?

choices.text: it argues that zoos should hold an even larger number of animals
choices.rationale: Text 2 opposes confinement.

choices.text: it agrees that zoos are truly the only option for inspiring the public
choices.rationale: Text 2 rejects the 'only option' view.

choices.text: it offers alternatives that, it claims, achieve the benefit without confinement
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 proposes documentaries and reserves as substitutes.

choices.text: it denies that members of the public care about wild animals at all
choices.rationale: Text 2 does not deny public interest.

explanation: Text 2 counters by proposing confinement-free alternatives.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Modern zoos fund conservation and let millions of people encounter animals they would never see in the wild, building public support for protecting habitats. That connection, supporters say, is hard to create any other way.
stimulus.text_b: Even well-run zoos confine animals to spaces far smaller than their natural ranges. The author contends that documentaries and protected reserves can inspire the public without keeping individual animals in enclosures.

prompt: Based on the texts, the author of Text 1 and the author of Text 2 would most likely disagree about whether

choices.text: zoos are the best available way to build public support for conservation
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 calls the zoo connection hard to replace; Text 2 says reserves and films can do it.

choices.text: animals can ever be seen by the public in filmed nature documentaries
choices.rationale: Both accept documentaries exist; that is not the dispute.

choices.text: zoos, as a simple matter of fact, keep animals housed within enclosures
choices.rationale: Both agree zoos contain animals.

choices.text: protecting natural habitats from harm is a desirable goal worth pursuing
choices.rationale: Both value habitat protection.

explanation: Their disagreement is whether zoos are the best route to public support.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Modern zoos fund conservation and let millions of people encounter animals they would never see in the wild, building public support for protecting habitats. That connection, supporters say, is hard to create any other way.
stimulus.text_b: Even well-run zoos confine animals to spaces far smaller than their natural ranges. The author contends that documentaries and protected reserves can inspire the public without keeping individual animals in enclosures.

prompt: Which choice best describes the relationship between the two texts?

choices.text: they reach opposing conclusions about homework in the early grades
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 1 favors early homework; Text 2 opposes it.

choices.text: they fully agree on the clear value of homework in the early grades
choices.rationale: They disagree rather than agree.

choices.text: Text 2 mainly offers evidence that supports the conclusion of Text 1
choices.rationale: Text 2 undercuts, not supports, Text 1.

choices.text: both texts argue that the amount of homework should be increased
choices.rationale: Text 2 argues for less, not more.

explanation: The texts take opposing positions on the same question.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Assigning homework in the early grades teaches children responsibility and reinforces the day's lessons. A short, regular task helps young students build the habit of independent work long before the stakes are high.
stimulus.text_b: For young children, nightly homework offers little measurable benefit and can sour their attitude toward school. Time spent on worksheets, the author argues, would be better spent reading for pleasure or playing outside.

prompt: How would the author of Text 2 most likely respond to the claim in Text 1 that homework 'reinforces the day's lessons'?

choices.text: by insisting that young children should be assigned more worksheets, not fewer
choices.rationale: Text 2 favors reading and play over worksheets.

choices.text: by agreeing that homework is clearly essential in the early grades for every child
choices.rationale: Text 2 disagrees that homework is essential for the young.

choices.text: by claiming that homework has no effect on responsibility at any age whatsoever
choices.rationale: Text 2 addresses early grades, not 'any age,' so this overstates its claim.

choices.text: by arguing that the benefit is small and is outweighed by drawbacks for young children
choices.rationale: Correct. Text 2 says the benefit is little and the costs (souring attitudes) are real.

explanation: Text 2 would counter that the reinforcing benefit is minor and outweighed for young children.

stimulus.title: Paired passages
stimulus.text: Assigning homework in the early grades teaches children responsibility and reinforces the day's lessons. A short, regular task helps young students build the habit of independent work long before the stakes are high.
stimulus.text_b: For young children, nightly homework offers little measurable benefit and can sour their attitude toward school. Time spent on worksheets, the author argues, would be better spent reading for pleasure or playing outside.
